D) The bank's competitors have also begun installing automated kiosks.
E) The two remaining human tellers are among the bank's most experienced employees.
items.explanation: This is a plan-will-work strengthener. The kiosks only reduce average wait time if a large share of customers can actually use them; otherwise most people still queue for the two remaining tellers, making waits worse. Choice B secures exactly that assumption. A concerns cost, not wait time. C is a minor preference that does not bear on aggregate wait times. D is about competitors. E might slightly help teller-served customers but does not address whether the kiosks reduce the overall average, which depends on how many customers the kiosks can serve.

items.stem: Economist: Over the past decade, countries that lowered their corporate tax rates saw faster growth in business investment than countries that did not. Our nation should therefore lower its corporate tax rate in order to spur business investment. Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the economist's recommendation?
choices: A) Business investment is only one of several factors that influence a country's overall economic output.
B) Lowering the corporate tax rate would reduce government revenue in the short term.
C) Some countries that did not lower corporate tax rates nevertheless experienced modest growth in business investment.

D) The countries that lowered their corporate tax rates and the countries that did not were, before the change, similar in their levels of business investment, workforce skills, and political stability.
E) The economist's nation currently has a higher corporate tax rate than the global average.
items.explanation: The recommendation rests on transferring an observed cross-country pattern to this nation, which assumes the tax cut, not pre-existing differences, drove the faster investment growth. Choice B strengthens by establishing that the two groups of countries were otherwise comparable beforehand, so the tax change is the plausible differentiator and the analogy to this nation holds. A weakens the broader payoff by noting investment is not everything. C is a cost, which does not strengthen. D is consistent with tax cuts helping but does not rule out other causes. E establishes room to cut but not that cutting will produce the investment effect.

items.stem: A publisher found that novels with a person's face on the cover sold better, on average, than novels with abstract art on the cover. The marketing director concluded that putting a face on the cover of the publisher's upcoming thriller will increase its sales relative to using abstract art. Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the marketing director's conclusion?
choices: A) The publisher's competitors mostly use abstract art on their thriller covers.
B) Abstract cover art is generally cheaper to license than photographs of faces.
C) The upcoming thriller was written by a first-time author.
D) Readers surveyed said they found faces on covers more eye-catching than abstract art.
E) The novels with faces on their covers and the novels with abstract art on their covers were, as groups, comparable in genre, author popularity, and marketing budget.
items.explanation: The director generalizes a past sales difference to predict a future one, which assumes the cover art, not other differences between the book groups, caused the sales gap. Choice C strengthens by showing the two groups were otherwise comparable (genre, author popularity, budget), so the cover type is the plausible cause and the prediction is warranted. E is a survey of stated preference, which is weaker than actual sales behavior and does not rule out confounders. A is about competitors. B is a cost consideration. D introduces a difference (first-time author) that, if anything, complicates the generalization.

items.stem: Researcher: In a survey conducted at three luxury health resorts, 85 percent of respondents said they exercise at least five times per week. We conclude that at least 85 percent of adults in the country exercise at least five times per week. Which of the following, if true, would most strengthen the researcher's conclusion?
choices: A) People who visit luxury health resorts do not, on average, exercise more frequently than the adult population of the country as a whole.
B) The survey used a clearly worded questionnaire that respondents found easy to understand.
C) The three health resorts are located in different regions of the country.
D) The survey included several hundred respondents at each resort.
E) Most respondents completed the survey without assistance from resort staff.
items.explanation: This is a sampling-representativeness strengthener. The fatal flaw is that people at luxury health resorts are likely far more exercise-oriented than the general population, so the sample is biased. Choice C directly neutralizes that bias by asserting resort-goers exercise no more than the population average, making the extrapolation defensible. A (clear wording), D (large sample), and E (unassisted responses) all improve data quality but do nothing about the biased selection of who was sampled. B spreads the bias geographically but the respondents are still resort-goers, so the sample remains unrepresentative.

items.stem: Over the past five years, neighborhoods with more coffee shops have also had higher average home prices. A real estate blogger argued that the presence of coffee shops raises nearby home prices, and recommended that towns encourage coffee shops in order to boost home values. Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the argument that coffee shops raise home prices, rather than the reverse?
choices: A) Coffee shops tend to open in neighborhoods only after home prices there have already begun to rise.
B) In several neighborhoods, home prices rose in the year after new coffee shops opened, whereas comparable neighborhoods without new coffee shops saw no such rise in the same year.
C) Coffee shops in wealthy neighborhoods tend to charge higher prices for their drinks.
D) Residents of neighborhoods with many coffee shops report high satisfaction with their neighborhoods.
E) Some homebuyers say they consider the availability of nearby amenities when choosing where to live.
items.explanation: The main alternative explanation is reverse causation: rising home prices attract coffee shops rather than the other way around. Choice B strengthens by establishing temporal order (coffee shops open, then prices rise) against a control group, supporting the causal direction the blogger claims. A explicitly asserts the reverse direction, so it weakens. C and D are about drink prices and satisfaction, not the direction of the price effect. E states a general preference but does not establish that coffee shops precede and cause price increases in these neighborhoods.

items.stem: Archaeologist: The ancient granary we excavated shows fire damage on its lower walls but not its upper walls. I hypothesize that the granary was destroyed by a grain-dust fire that started at floor level. Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the archaeologist's hypothesis?
choices: A) The granary was located near a river that occasionally flooded.
B) Other buildings at the site show fire damage on their upper walls.
C) The granary was built primarily of stone rather than wood.
D) Grain dust is highly flammable and, when ignited, tends to burn most intensely near the ground where the dust settles.
E) The excavation of the granary took several months to complete.
items.explanation: This is a best-explanation strengthener that works by supplying a plausible mechanism matching the evidence. The damage pattern (lower walls burned, upper walls not) is explained if the fire burned hottest near the floor. Choice A supplies exactly that mechanism for grain-dust fires, tightly fitting the observed pattern. B (flooding) suggests a competing, non-fire cause. C describes other buildings with the opposite pattern and does not support this hypothesis. D (stone construction) is neutral to where the fire started. E concerns excavation logistics, not the cause of the damage.

items.stem: Manufacturer: Our new factory robot can assemble a unit in half the time a human worker takes. Therefore, replacing our human assemblers with these robots will at least double the number of units our factory produces per day. Which of the following is an assumption on which the argument depends, such that establishing it would most strengthen the argument?
choices: A) The robots are more expensive to purchase than the annual wages of the workers they replace.
B) Customers will be willing to buy the additional units the factory produces.
C) The robots require occasional maintenance by trained technicians.
D) Human assemblers occasionally make errors that must be corrected.
E) Assembly, rather than some other stage such as supplying parts or packaging, is the step that currently limits how many units the factory can produce per day.
items.explanation: The argument leaps from doubling assembly speed to doubling total daily output, which only follows if assembly is the current bottleneck. If another stage (parts supply or packaging) limits output, faster assembly will not double production. Choice C states and secures this required assumption, strengthening the argument. A and D are costs, irrelevant to output volume. B concerns demand, not production capacity. E notes human errors, which would suggest robots help quality but does not establish that assembly speed governs total output.

items.stem: A school district is considering lengthening the school day by one hour to raise student test scores. Critics note that tired students may learn less in the extra hour. Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the case that lengthening the school day will raise test scores despite the critics' concern?
choices: A) In a pilot program, the added hour was scheduled in the morning, when students are most alert, and pilot students' test scores rose significantly relative to a comparison group.
B) Lengthening the school day would increase the district's spending on utilities and staff.
C) Some parents would prefer that the school day remain the same length.
D) Students in the district currently score near the national average on standardized tests.
E) Teachers would need additional training to make use of the extra hour.
items.explanation: This strengthener must both support the plan and defuse a stated counter-consideration (tired students). Choice C does both: scheduling the extra hour in the morning avoids the fatigue objection, and the pilot's measured score gain against a comparison group provides direct evidence the plan works. A and E are costs. B is a preference unrelated to test outcomes. D describes current standing but gives no evidence the longer day improves scores or addresses tiredness.

items.stem: A wildlife agency reintroduced wolves into a park in 2015. Since then, the park's beaver population has grown substantially. A biologist claims the wolves caused the beaver increase: wolves reduced the elk population, which allowed streamside willow (the beavers' main food and building material) to recover. Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the biologist's causal chain?
choices: A) Beavers are known to be industrious builders of dams and lodges.
B) After the wolves were reintroduced, the elk population declined and streamside willow grew noticeably taller and denser than before.
C) The park's total area did not change after 2015.
D) Wolves occasionally prey on animals other than elk.
E) The beaver population in a neighboring park, which has no wolves and no elk, also grew after 2015.
items.explanation: The claim is a multi-step causal chain (wolves reduce elk, fewer elk allow willow to recover, more willow supports more beavers). Strengthening requires evidence that the intermediate links actually occurred. Choice B confirms two crucial links: elk declined and willow recovered, exactly as the chain predicts. A is a generic fact about beavers. C rules out one minor alternative but does not confirm the mechanism. D concerns other prey, tangential to the elk link. E is a confounder that weakens the claim, since beavers grew elsewhere without wolves or elk, suggesting a different, park-independent cause.

items.stem: Columnist: A recent policy allowing employees to work from home two days a week was followed by a 15 percent rise in the company's overall productivity. Each of the following, if true, strengthens the claim that the work-from-home policy caused the productivity rise EXCEPT:
choices: A) Employees reported spending less time commuting and more time on work tasks after the policy took effect.
B) A rival firm that adopted an identical work-from-home policy saw a similar rise in productivity.
C) The company introduced no other significant changes to its operations during the period in question.
D) During the same period, the company upgraded all employees' computers to substantially faster models.
E) Departments whose employees worked from home more often showed larger productivity gains than departments whose employees worked from home less often.
items.explanation: In a strengthen-EXCEPT question, four choices support the causal link and the answer is the one that does not (here, it undermines the link by offering a competing cause). Choice E introduces a simultaneous computer upgrade, a rival explanation for the productivity gain, so it fails to strengthen and is the correct answer. A supplies a mechanism (more work time). B is a corroborating parallel case. C rules out other changes. D shows a dose-response pattern (more work-from-home means bigger gains), all of which strengthen the causal claim.

items.stem: A gym owner noticed that members who attend group fitness classes renew their memberships at a higher rate than members who work out alone. The owner concluded that attending group classes makes members more likely to renew, and plans to encourage more members to join classes. Which of the following, if true, most strengthens the owner's conclusion?
choices: A) Group fitness classes require the gym to hire additional instructors.
B) Some members who work out alone say they prefer exercising without a schedule.
C) The gym offers a wide variety of group fitness classes at different times of day.
D) Members who attend group classes tend to visit the gym at busier times.
E) When members who had worked out alone were persuaded to start attending group classes, their subsequent renewal rate rose to match that of longtime class attendees.
items.explanation: The conclusion assumes classes cause renewal rather than, say, already-committed members self-selecting into classes. Choice D strengthens most powerfully by showing that when solo members switched to classes, their renewal rate rose, evidence that the behavior change itself produces the outcome and countering the self-selection alternative. A is a cost. B and E describe differences between the groups without establishing causation, and E if anything hints at a confounder (crowd/timing). C describes class variety but gives no evidence that attending raises renewal.

items.stem: Every applicant to the Meridian Fellowship who submitted a portfolio was granted an interview. No applicant who was granted an interview had a criminal record on file. If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?
choices: A) Every applicant who submitted a portfolio had no criminal record on file.
B) Some applicants who submitted a portfolio had a criminal record on file.
C) Every applicant who was granted an interview submitted a portfolio.
D) An applicant with a criminal record on file was granted an interview.
E) No applicant who was granted an interview submitted a portfolio.
items.explanation: Chain the two conditionals: submitted portfolio -> granted interview, and granted interview -> no criminal record on file. Therefore submitted portfolio -> no criminal record on file, which is exactly (A). (B) contradicts the chain. (C) reverses the first conditional (affirming the consequent). (D) directly contradicts the second statement. (E) contradicts the first statement.

items.stem: In a town survey of 500 residents, 320 reported owning a car and 210 reported owning a bicycle. Each resident answered both questions. Based only on this information, which of the following must be true about the number of residents who own both a car and a bicycle?
choices: A) Exactly 30 residents own both a car and a bicycle.
B) At least 30 residents own both a car and a bicycle.
C) At most 30 residents own both a car and a bicycle.
D) No residents own both a car and a bicycle.
E) At least 210 residents own both a car and a bicycle.
items.explanation: By inclusion-exclusion, owners of at least one item is at most 500, so both >= 320 + 210 - 500 = 30. The overlap could be larger (up to 210 if all bicycle owners also own cars), so only 'at least 30' is guaranteed; (A) is correct. (B) fixes a value that is only the minimum. (C) inverts the bound. (D) contradicts the forced minimum. (E) confuses the maximum possible overlap with a guaranteed minimum.

items.stem: At a distribution center, any shipment that is flagged by the scanner is sent for manual inspection. No shipment that was sent for manual inspection was released from the center on Monday. Which of the following can be properly concluded from these statements?
choices: A) Any shipment not flagged by the scanner was released on Monday.
B) Every shipment sent for manual inspection had been flagged by the scanner.
C) Some flagged shipments were released on Monday.
D) Any shipment released from the center on Monday was not flagged by the scanner.
E) No shipment was flagged by the scanner on Monday.
items.explanation: Flagged -> inspected, and inspected -> not released Monday. Contrapositive of the chain: released Monday -> not inspected -> not flagged, which is (A). (B) affirms a converse and adds unsupported content. (C) reverses the first conditional. (D) contradicts the chain. (E) is about when flagging occurred, which is not addressed.

items.stem: Clinic A treated 400 patients last month, of whom 15 percent tested positive for allergy Q. Clinic B treated 250 patients over the same period, of whom 24 percent tested positive for allergy Q. No patient was treated at both clinics. Which of the following is most strongly supported by the information above?
choices: A) Clinic B recorded more patients testing positive for allergy Q than Clinic A did.
B) Allergy Q is more common in the population served by Clinic A.
C) Clinic A treated more patients testing positive for allergy Q than Clinic B did.
D) Combined, the two clinics recorded fewer than 100 patients testing positive for allergy Q.
E) The two clinics recorded the same number of patients testing positive for allergy Q.
items.explanation: Clinic A: 15% of 400 = 60 positives. Clinic B: 24% of 250 = 60 positives. The absolute counts are equal, so (A) is supported. (B) and (D) both assert an inequality that does not hold (60 = 60). (C) confuses a higher rate at B with a claim about A and reaches beyond the data. (E) is false: 60 + 60 = 120, which is not fewer than 100.

items.stem: A software team's policy states that a feature is released to users only if it has passed both the security review and the performance review. The 'Quicksort' feature was released to users last week. Which of the following must be true about the Quicksort feature?
choices: A) It passed both the security review and the performance review.
B) It passed the security review but may have failed the performance review.
C) It passed the performance review but not the security review.
D) It passed at least one of the two reviews.
E) It was the only feature released last week.
items.explanation: 'Released only if it passed both reviews' means released -> passed security AND passed performance. Since Quicksort was released, it passed both; (B) is correct. (A) and (C) allow one review to be failed, which contradicts the 'both' requirement. (D) understates the requirement (both are needed, not just one). (E) is unsupported by anything in the statement.

items.stem: A regional bakery chain reports that among its 12 branches, every branch that offers a delivery service also offers weekend hours, and no branch located outside the downtown zone offers weekend hours. Branch 7 offers a delivery service. Which of the following must be true of Branch 7?
choices: A) Branch 7 does not offer weekend hours.
B) Branch 7 is located in the downtown zone.
C) Branch 7 is one of exactly six downtown branches.
D) Every branch in the downtown zone offers a delivery service.
E) Branch 7 is the only branch that offers a delivery service.
items.explanation: Delivery -> weekend hours, and outside-downtown -> no weekend hours (equivalently, weekend hours -> downtown). Branch 7 offers delivery, so it offers weekend hours, so it must be downtown; (A) is correct. (B) contradicts the delivery-to-weekend-hours link. (C) invents a specific count. (D) reverses the first conditional. (E) asserts uniqueness that nothing supports.

items.stem: A university committee found that all students enrolled in the Advanced Robotics seminar had previously completed Linear Algebra, and that every student who completed Linear Algebra had scored at least a B in Calculus. Priya is enrolled in the Advanced Robotics seminar. Which of the following must be true?
choices: A) Priya is a robotics major.
B) Priya completed Linear Algebra after enrolling in the seminar.
C) Every student who scored at least a B in Calculus is in the seminar.
D) Priya scored at least a B in Calculus.
E) Priya scored exactly a B in Calculus.
items.explanation: Enrolled in seminar -> completed Linear Algebra -> scored at least a B in Calculus. Priya is enrolled, so she scored at least a B in Calculus; (A) must be true. (B) introduces a major not mentioned. (C) invents timing information. (D) reverses the chain (affirming the consequent). (E) narrows 'at least a B' to 'exactly a B,' which is not guaranteed.

items.stem: Naturalist: On Espen Island, the population of ground beetles is larger in every year that follows a wet winter than in any year following a dry winter. Ground beetles are the sole food source of the island's shrew population, and shrews cannot survive a year in which the beetle population falls below a fixed threshold. Last year followed a dry winter, and the shrew population survived. Which of the following is most strongly supported?
choices: A) The beetle population last year was exactly at the survival threshold.
B) Shrews will go extinct after the next dry winter.
C) A wet winter guarantees an increase in the shrew population.
D) Beetles are more numerous than shrews on Espen Island.
E) Following a wet winter, the beetle population is also above the survival threshold for shrews.
items.explanation: Last year followed a dry winter and shrews survived, so the beetle population was at or above the threshold in that dry-following year. Beetle populations are larger after a wet winter than after any dry-following year, so in a wet-following year the population is even higher, hence also above the threshold; (A) is supported. (B) fixes an exact value that need not hold (the beetles could have exceeded the threshold). (C) is an unsupported prediction. (D) overstates the link between beetles and shrew growth. (E) compares raw counts the passage never gives.

items.stem: Advertisement: Over the past decade, the number of households in Grantwood that own at least one electric vehicle has tripled, while the town's total number of households has grown by only 20 percent. Which of the following can be most reliably inferred from these figures alone?
choices: A) The proportion of Grantwood households owning at least one electric vehicle is now higher than it was a decade ago.
B) Most households in Grantwood now own an electric vehicle.
C) The number of gasoline-powered vehicles in Grantwood has declined.
D) Electric vehicles have become cheaper in Grantwood over the decade.
E) The average Grantwood household now owns more than one electric vehicle.
items.explanation: The count of EV-owning households multiplied by 3 while total households multiplied by only 1.2, so the ratio of EV-owning households to all households rose (3 > 1.2); (A) is a reliable inference. (B) requires an absolute majority the figures never establish. (C) concerns gasoline vehicles, which are not mentioned. (D) offers a price cause that is unsupported. (E) confuses households owning at least one EV with vehicles-per-household.

items.stem: A logistics manager notes: 'Of our 50 delivery routes, more than half run through the harbor district, and more than half include an overnight stop.' Which of the following must be true?
choices: A) Exactly half of the routes both run through the harbor district and include an overnight stop.
B) At least one route both runs through the harbor district and includes an overnight stop.
C) No route both runs through the harbor district and includes an overnight stop.
D) Every route that runs through the harbor district also includes an overnight stop.
E) More routes include an overnight stop than run through the harbor district.
items.explanation: More than half means at least 26 of 50 routes run through the harbor district and at least 26 include an overnight stop. If these two groups were disjoint, they would total at least 52 routes, exceeding 50; therefore they must overlap in at least one route, so (A) must be true. (B) fixes an exact overlap not implied. (C) contradicts the forced overlap. (D) asserts full containment that is not required. (E) compares the two counts, but both are only bounded below by 26, so no order between them is forced.

items.stem: Museum guide: No exhibit in the east wing may be photographed with a flash. Every exhibit that is more than 300 years old is displayed in the east wing. The bronze urn is more than 300 years old. Which of the following must be true of the bronze urn?
choices: A) It may be photographed, but only without a flash.
B) It is the oldest exhibit in the museum.
C) It is not displayed in the east wing.
D) It may not be photographed with a flash.
E) It may be photographed with a flash if permission is obtained.
items.explanation: More than 300 years old -> displayed in the east wing -> may not be photographed with a flash. The urn is more than 300 years old, so it may not be photographed with a flash; (C) is required. (A) adds a permission for flash-free photography that is not stated. (B) claims a superlative age not supported. (D) contradicts the second conditional. (E) directly contradicts the flash prohibition.

items.stem: A regional bakery chain replaced the sugar in its muffins with a plant-based sweetener, expecting that this change would attract health-conscious customers and thereby increase muffin sales at its stores. Which of the following would be most useful to know in evaluating whether the change is likely to increase muffin sales?
choices: A) Whether the plant-based sweetener costs the bakery more per muffin than sugar did
B) Whether the bakery's competitors also sell muffins
C) Whether the bakery advertises its muffins on local radio
D) Whether the bakery's founder personally prefers the taste of the new sweetener
E) Whether health-conscious customers who avoided the muffins because of their sugar content would find the new muffins acceptable
items.explanation: The prediction is that the switch will attract health-conscious customers and raise sales. The most useful thing to evaluate is whether the targeted group (health-conscious people who previously avoided the muffins) will actually accept the new product. If they would, the plan can work; if not, it cannot. A concerns cost, not sales. C, D, and E are irrelevant to whether the specific change drives the predicted sales increase.

items.stem: After a mid-sized city installed brighter LED streetlights along its downtown streets, the number of nighttime pedestrian injuries reported downtown fell by 30 percent the following year. City officials concluded that the brighter lighting caused the decline and plan to install the lights citywide. The answer to which of the following questions would be most useful in evaluating the officials' conclusion?
choices: A) Whether the total number of pedestrians walking downtown at night changed substantially over the same period
B) Whether the LED streetlights consume less electricity than the lights they replaced
C) Whether residents of the city generally approve of the new streetlights
D) Whether the new streetlights were manufactured locally
E) Whether other cities have installed similar LED streetlights
items.explanation: The conclusion is causal: brighter lights caused fewer injuries. A key alternative explanation is a change in exposure — if far fewer people walked downtown at night, injuries could fall for reasons unrelated to lighting. So knowing whether pedestrian volume changed is most useful. A and D concern the lights' other properties, not the injury effect. B is about opinion. E is about other cities, not this causal claim.

items.stem: A publishing company argues that switching its magazine from monthly to weekly publication will increase its total annual advertising revenue, because advertisers pay per issue and there would be 52 issues per year instead of 12. In evaluating this argument, it would be most useful to determine which of the following?
choices: A) Whether the company's editorial staff can produce quality content on a weekly schedule
B) Whether the amount advertisers are willing to pay per issue would remain roughly the same after the switch to weekly publication
C) Whether readers prefer receiving the magazine monthly rather than weekly
D) Whether the company's printing costs would rise under weekly publication
E) Whether rival magazines publish weekly or monthly
items.explanation: Total ad revenue equals price per issue times number of issues. The argument assumes price per issue stays constant while multiplying issues from 12 to 52. If advertisers pay far less per issue for a weekly (e.g., because each issue reaches fewer readers or has less impact), total revenue might not rise. So B directly tests the load-bearing assumption. A concerns feasibility of content, not revenue. D concerns cost, not revenue. C and E are tangential to the revenue arithmetic.

items.stem: A pharmaceutical researcher notes that patients who took Drug X for six months showed, on average, lower blood pressure than patients who took a placebo over the same period. She concludes that Drug X lowers blood pressure. A colleague objects that the result might instead reflect that patients with milder conditions were more likely to stay on Drug X for the full six months. The answer to which of the following questions would be most useful in deciding whether the colleague's objection undermines the researcher's conclusion?
choices: A) Whether Drug X is more expensive than the placebo to produce
B) Whether the placebo group also showed some reduction in blood pressure
C) Whether Drug X has side effects unrelated to blood pressure
D) Whether patients were randomly assigned to receive Drug X or the placebo
E) Whether the six-month study period is standard for blood-pressure trials
items.explanation: The colleague's objection is a self-selection worry: milder patients stayed on Drug X, biasing the comparison. Random assignment to the drug or placebo, if done properly, would neutralize this concern because assignment would not depend on illness severity. So A directly tests whether the objection has force. C describes a result but does not address selection bias. B, D, and E are irrelevant to whether the group comparison is biased by who stayed on the drug.

items.stem: An online retailer offers free two-day shipping to members of its paid loyalty program. Program members spend, on average, three times as much per year as non-members. The retailer's marketing director argues that expanding the program by lowering its membership fee would substantially raise the company's total revenue. Which of the following would be most useful to establish in evaluating the marketing director's argument?
choices: A) Whether the retailer's warehouses can handle faster shipping volumes
B) Whether non-members are aware that a loyalty program exists
C) Whether the current membership fee covers the cost of free shipping
D) Whether members are more satisfied with customer service than non-members
E) Whether members' high spending is caused by the program's benefits or instead reflects that heavy spenders are the ones who choose to join
items.explanation: The argument treats membership as the cause of high spending, so adding members should add high spenders. But the correlation could run the other way: people who already spend a lot are the ones who join. If so, lowering the fee to add casual shoppers would not turn them into big spenders, and revenue would not rise as expected. A tests exactly this cause-versus-selection issue. D concerns cost, not the revenue prediction. B, C, and E do not bear on whether new members would spend like current ones.

items.stem: To reduce employee turnover, a company introduced a policy allowing any employee to work remotely up to three days a week. In the year after the policy began, turnover among the company's software engineers dropped sharply, and management credited the remote-work policy. Which of the following, if determined, would be most useful in evaluating whether the drop in engineer turnover should be attributed to the remote-work policy?
choices: A) Whether turnover among the company's non-engineering staff, who were equally eligible for remote work, also dropped sharply
B) Whether the software engineers were satisfied with their salaries before the policy began
C) Whether the remote-work policy increased the engineers' daily productivity
D) Whether the company's engineers had requested a remote-work policy in the past
E) Whether competing firms offer remote work to their engineers
items.explanation: To attribute the turnover drop to the remote-work policy, one wants to rule out other company-wide or industry-wide causes. Non-engineering staff were equally eligible for remote work. If their turnover also dropped sharply, that is consistent with the policy helping everyone; if their turnover did NOT drop despite equal eligibility, that suggests something specific to engineers (e.g., a hot job market cooling) rather than the shared policy explains the change. Either way, the comparison is highly diagnostic. C measures productivity, not turnover. B, D, and E do not isolate the policy's effect on turnover.

items.stem: A school district plans to lengthen the school day by one hour, predicting that the extra instructional time will raise students' standardized test scores. In evaluating this prediction, it would be most useful to know which of the following?
choices: A) Whether teachers support lengthening the school day
B) Whether the extra hour would be used for additional instruction rather than for lunch, recess, or study hall
C) Whether the district's buses can accommodate a later dismissal time
D) Whether nearby districts have longer or shorter school days
E) Whether the district's budget can cover the cost of a longer day
items.explanation: The prediction rests on the assumption that the extra hour actually becomes extra instruction. If the added hour is spent on lunch, recess, or study hall, there is no additional instructional time and the predicted score gain has no basis. A tests this assumption directly. B, C, and E concern feasibility or cost, not whether scores would rise. D is a comparison that does not bear on the causal prediction.

items.stem: A conservation group claims that a proposed dam will harm a river's trout population, citing that a similar dam built on a neighboring river was followed by a collapse of that river's trout population. A dam engineer responds that the two rivers differ in ways that make the comparison unreliable. The answer to which of the following questions would most help in assessing whether the engineer's response weakens the conservation group's argument?
choices: A) Whether the proposed dam would generate more electricity than the neighboring river's dam
B) Whether the conservation group has opposed dams in the past
C) Whether trout are the most economically valuable fish in the river under consideration
D) Whether the neighboring river's trout collapse was caused by the dam or by a factor absent from the river now under consideration
E) Whether the neighboring river's dam was more expensive to build than the proposed dam
items.explanation: The conservation group reasons by analogy from the neighboring river. The engineer's response is that relevant differences make the analogy unreliable. The most useful thing to determine is whether the neighboring collapse was actually caused by that dam or by some other factor not present in the new case — if the collapse had a different cause, the analogy fails and the engineer is right. A targets this. B and E concern cost or power output, not the trout effect. C is an ad hominem consideration. D concerns economic value, not the biological prediction.

items.stem: A coffee company's data show that in the three months after it began playing upbeat music in its cafes, average customer spending per visit rose by 12 percent. The company concludes that the upbeat music caused customers to spend more and plans to keep it playing indefinitely. To evaluate this conclusion, it would be most useful to determine whether
choices: A) customers reported that they enjoyed the upbeat music
B) the upbeat music was more expensive to license than the previous music
C) the company's employees preferred working with the upbeat music playing
D) the company plays the same music in all of its cafes
E) the company also raised its menu prices at the start of the three-month period
items.explanation: Average spending per visit could rise for reasons other than music. A menu price increase at the same time would raise spending per visit independent of any music effect, offering an alternative explanation for the 12 percent. So A is most useful. B and D measure enjoyment, not whether music, rather than something else, drove spending. C concerns cost. E concerns consistency of playlist, not the cause of the spending increase.

items.stem: A nonprofit sends fundraising letters and finds that letters printed on recycled paper generated a higher average donation than letters printed on standard paper. The nonprofit's director concludes that mentioning the recycled paper in future letters will increase donations. Which of the following would be most useful to know in evaluating the director's conclusion?
choices: A) Whether recipients of the recycled-paper letters could tell, or were told, that the paper was recycled
B) Whether recycled paper costs the nonprofit more than standard paper
C) Whether the nonprofit sent more recycled-paper letters than standard-paper letters
D) Whether donors prefer email solicitations to printed letters
E) Whether the nonprofit's mission is related to environmental causes
items.explanation: The director's plan is to MENTION the recycled paper to boost donations. That only works if recipients' awareness of the recycled paper is what drove the higher donations. If recipients could not tell the paper was recycled, then the paper type was not what influenced them, and mentioning it may do nothing. A tests whether awareness is the operative factor. B concerns cost. C concerns volume, not average donation. D and E are tangential to whether mentioning the paper helps.

items.stem: A logistics firm is deciding between two delivery routes for a daily shipment. Route A is 40 kilometers and passes through the city center; Route B is 55 kilometers on a highway bypass. The operations manager argues that switching from Route A to Route B will reduce the average daily delivery time. Which of the following would be most useful to determine in evaluating the manager's argument?
choices: A) Whether Route B has more scenic views than Route A
B) Whether the average speed a truck can maintain on Route B is high enough to offset its greater distance compared with the speed achievable on Route A
C) Whether Route A is shorter than Route B
D) Whether the firm's trucks are fuel-efficient enough for a 55-kilometer route
E) Whether drivers are more familiar with Route A than with Route B
items.explanation: Delivery time depends on both distance and speed. Route B is longer but on a highway; the argument that it is faster only holds if the higher achievable speed on Route B more than compensates for the extra 15 kilometers, relative to the slower city-center speed on Route A. B tests exactly this trade-off, which determines the time comparison. C merely restates given distance facts. A and D concern scenery and fuel, not time. E might matter marginally but does not directly settle the speed-vs-distance comparison.

items.stem: A tech startup found that job applicants who were referred by current employees stayed at the company longer, on average, than applicants who applied without a referral. The head of hiring concludes that expanding the employee-referral program will reduce overall employee turnover. The answer to which of the following questions would be most useful in evaluating this conclusion?
choices: A) Whether current employees would be willing to refer more applicants
B) Whether referred applicants are paid more than non-referred applicants at hiring
C) Whether the company's turnover is higher or lower than the industry average
D) Whether referred applicants tend to differ from non-referred applicants in characteristics, such as skill or prior job stability, that themselves affect how long someone stays
E) Whether the referral program offers cash bonuses to referring employees
items.explanation: The conclusion assumes referral itself causes longer tenure, so more referrals means less turnover. But referred applicants might simply be a different kind of person — more skilled or with steadier job histories — and those traits, not the referral, could explain longer tenure. If so, expanding referrals need not lower turnover. A tests whether the two groups are comparable, the crux of the causal inference. C is one specific confound but narrower than A. B concerns feasibility. D and E do not address whether referral drives retention.

items.stem: A gym owner plans to add a fleet of new rowing machines, reasoning that because the gym's existing rowing machines are almost always in use during peak hours, adding more will increase the total number of member visits during those hours. Which of the following would be most useful to know in evaluating the gym owner's reasoning?
choices: A) Whether the new rowing machines are more expensive than the existing ones
B) Whether the rowing machines are manufactured by a well-known brand
C) Whether the gym offers group fitness classes in addition to equipment
D) Whether the gym's treadmills are also heavily used during peak hours
E) Whether members who currently cannot use a rowing machine at peak times are choosing not to come to the gym at those times as a result
items.explanation: The owner infers that fully-used rowing machines are turning away visits that more machines would capture. That only follows if some members are actually staying away at peak times because they cannot get a rowing machine. If unavailable rowing machines are not causing anyone to skip visits (e.g., members just use other equipment), adding machines would not raise total visits. C tests this directly. A and B concern cost and brand. D and E describe other offerings but do not show that rower shortages suppress visits.

items.stem: The Harborline commuter ferry recently cut the fare for its 7 a.m. crossing by 20 percent, expecting the cheaper fare to attract more riders. In the following two months, however, the number of passengers on the 7 a.m. crossing actually declined. Which of the following, if true, best explains the decline in passengers?
choices: A) Shortly before the fare cut, a new subway line opened that carried commuters downtown faster and more cheaply than the ferry.
B) The ferry operator did not change the fares on any of its other daily crossings.
C) The ferry's 7 a.m. crossing had historically been its most popular departure of the day.
D) The fare cut was widely advertised on posters at the ferry terminal.
E) The cost of operating the ferry rose slightly during the two-month period.
items.explanation: The paradox is that a lower fare was followed by fewer riders. An explanation must supply a countervailing force strong enough to outweigh the fare cut. (B) introduces a superior competing option that appeared at the same time, drawing riders away despite the lower fare - this resolves the discrepancy. (A) is irrelevant to why 7 a.m. ridership fell. (C) about past popularity does not explain a decline now. (D) advertising a cut should raise, not lower, ridership, deepening the paradox. (E) operating cost affects profit, not passenger counts.

items.stem: A city installed automated speed cameras on Maple Boulevard. In the year after installation, the total number of traffic accidents on Maple Boulevard rose, even though the average speed of vehicles on that road fell sharply. Each of the following, if true, helps to explain this outcome EXCEPT:
choices: A) After the cameras were installed, many drivers braked abruptly upon seeing them, causing a large increase in rear-end collisions.
B) The speed cameras reduced the average speed of vehicles most on the stretches where accidents had always been rare.
C) Traffic volume on Maple Boulevard nearly doubled after a parallel highway was closed for repairs during the year.
D) Drivers slowing for the cameras created stop-and-go conditions that led to more low-speed fender benders.
E) A confusing new lane-merge pattern was introduced on Maple Boulevard in the same year the cameras were installed.
items.explanation: We must find the choice that does NOT help reconcile 'lower speeds yet more accidents.' (A), (C) explain more accidents as a side effect of braking/slowing. (B) more traffic means more accident opportunities. (E) a separate hazard raises accidents. (D) says speed dropped most where accidents were already rare - this neither explains the rise in accidents nor supplies a new accident source, so it fails to resolve the paradox and is the correct EXCEPT answer.

items.stem: At Verano Foods, the plant that packages frozen vegetables reported that in every month of last year its rate of defective packages (defects per thousand packages) was lower than that of the competing plant at Nordell Foods. Yet over the full year, Verano's overall defect rate was higher than Nordell's. Assuming all figures are accurate, which of the following must be true?
choices: A) Verano packaged fewer total packages over the year than Nordell did.
B) Nordell's best month had a lower defect rate than Verano's best month.
C) Verano's defect rate rose steadily throughout the year.
D) At least one plant changed its defect rate substantially from month to month, and the two plants packaged very different numbers of packages in different months.
E) The two plants packaged the same total number of packages over the year.
items.explanation: This is Simpson's paradox: monthly rates can each favor Verano yet the annual pooled rate favor Nordell only if the plants' monthly volumes are weighted very differently across months (e.g., Verano produced most of its output in its higher-defect months). That requires both varying rates and disproportionate monthly volumes, so (B) must be true. (A) and (E) about totals need not hold; the effect is about the distribution of volume across months, not overall totals. (C) reverses the given monthly dominance. (D) 'steadily' is an unsupported specific pattern - the rise could be irregular.

items.stem: A nutrition columnist wrote: "People who drink diet soda are, on average, more likely to be overweight than people who drink no soda at all. This shows that diet soda causes weight gain." Which of the following, if true, most seriously undermines the columnist's causal explanation by offering a better account of the correlation?
choices: A) Diet sodas contain artificial sweeteners rather than sugar.
B) Some people who drink no soda consume large amounts of other sugary beverages.
C) The study measured soda consumption accurately using purchase records.
D) Diet soda has become more popular over the past decade.
E) People who are already overweight frequently switch to diet soda in an attempt to lose weight.
items.explanation: The correlation (diet-soda drinkers tend to be overweight) is presented as diet soda causing weight gain. The strongest alternative reverses the causation: being overweight leads people to choose diet soda. (B) provides exactly this reverse-causation account, so it best undermines the causal claim. (A) is a neutral fact about ingredients. (C) concerns a different group and does not explain the diet-soda link. (D) strengthens the data's reliability, not the causal challenge. (E) trend over time is irrelevant to the individual-level correlation.

items.stem: An electronics chain found that its stores in Region A had higher customer-satisfaction scores than its stores in Region B, yet Region A also had a much higher rate of product returns than Region B. Management wants a single explanation consistent with BOTH facts - higher satisfaction AND more returns in Region A. Which of the following, if true, best accomplishes this?
choices: A) Region A stores offer a generous no-questions-asked return policy that customers value highly and use freely.
B) Region B stores sell mostly inexpensive items that customers rarely bother to return.
C) Region A customers report being satisfied with the staff but dissatisfied with the products.
D) Region B stores have shorter checkout lines than Region A stores.
E) Region A has more stores than Region B.
items.explanation: The correct explanation must simultaneously produce higher satisfaction and more returns in Region A through one mechanism. (A) does this: a lenient return policy both delights customers (high satisfaction) and makes returns easy and frequent (high returns). (B) explains low returns in B but not high satisfaction in A, so it addresses only one fact. (C) explains returns but would lower satisfaction, contradicting a fact. (D) touches neither returns directly nor satisfaction consistently. (E) store count is a scaling factor that does not by itself explain higher rates.

items.stem: Two analysts propose explanations for a puzzle: Bluepeak Airlines raised its checked-bag fee, yet the total revenue it collected from bag fees fell the next quarter. Analyst 1 says fewer passengers flew Bluepeak that quarter. Analyst 2 says passengers responded to the higher fee by checking fewer bags. If it is later confirmed that Bluepeak carried MORE passengers that quarter than in the previous one, which of the following follows?
choices: A) Analyst 1's explanation is confirmed and Analyst 2's is refuted.
B) Analyst 1's explanation is refuted, and Analyst 2's remains a viable explanation.
E) Analyst 2's explanation is refuted, and Analyst 1's remains viable.
items.explanation: The new fact - more passengers flew - directly contradicts Analyst 1's claim that fewer passengers flew, so Analyst 1 is refuted. Analyst 2's account (each passenger checked fewer bags, cutting the number of paid bags enough to lower total fee revenue despite the higher per-bag fee and more passengers) is untouched and still viable. So (B). (A) and (D) wrongly keep or confirm Analyst 1. (C) wrongly refutes Analyst 2. (E) reverses which one survives.

items.stem: A pharmaceutical trial reported that a new drug reduced the risk of a certain infection in the patients who received it. Public-health officials were surprised to find that after the drug was approved and widely prescribed, the total number of these infections in the general population actually increased. Which of the following, if true, would by itself resolve this apparent contradiction WITHOUT questioning the accuracy of the trial?
choices: A) The trial enrolled only patients who were at unusually high risk of the infection.
B) The drug was more expensive than officials had anticipated.
C) The infection can be diagnosed only through a specialized laboratory test.
D) Because the drug was believed to be protective, many people who took it stopped using other preventive measures that had been more effective overall.
E) Some patients in the trial experienced mild side effects from the drug.
items.explanation: We need a resolution that keeps the trial valid (drug does reduce risk for a user, all else equal) yet explains a population-wide increase. (B) supplies a behavioral offset: reliance on the drug caused people to abandon a more effective precaution, so net risk rose across the population even though the drug itself helps - this reconciles both facts. (A) about trial enrollment would cast doubt on generalizability, i.e., question applicability rather than cleanly resolve. (C) cost does not affect infection counts among users. (D) diagnosis method does not create more real infections. (E) side effects are unrelated to infection incidence.

items.stem: A regional library system observed that ever since it began offering free e-book borrowing, the number of physical books checked out each month has risen substantially, even though many patrons now read e-books instead of print. The library expected physical checkouts to fall. Which of the following, if true, best explains why physical checkouts rose?
choices: A) The e-book service was funded by a one-time grant.
B) E-books can be borrowed without visiting a library branch.
C) The most popular titles are available in both e-book and print formats.
D) Some longtime patrons prefer the feel of physical books.
E) The free e-book service attracted many new library members, a large share of whom also borrow physical books.
items.explanation: The paradox: e-books were added, some patrons shifted to e-books, yet print checkouts rose. (B) resolves it by expanding the pool - the e-book service drew in many new members who also check out print, raising total print checkouts even if per-existing-patron print use fell. (A) funding source is irrelevant to checkout counts. (C) makes e-books convenient, which would tend to lower print checkouts, deepening the paradox. (D) format availability alone does not increase print borrowing. (E) explains why some print use persists but not an overall rise.

items.stem: Reviewers noticed a puzzle: Among restaurants listed on a popular review site, those with an average rating of exactly 4.0 stars tend to have HIGHER total revenue than those rated 4.8 stars. A commentator resolved the puzzle by pointing out a single unstated fact. Which of the following, if that unstated fact, best resolves the puzzle?
choices: A) The 4.8-star restaurants are overwhelmingly small, exclusive venues with few seats, while 4.0-star restaurants are typically large, high-volume establishments.
B) Diners generally prefer restaurants with higher ratings.
C) The review site displays ratings prominently on each restaurant's page.
D) A 4.8-star rating is harder to achieve than a 4.0-star rating.
E) Some 4.0-star restaurants have received a very large number of reviews.
items.explanation: The task is to identify the hidden fact that reconciles 'lower-rated restaurants earn more.' (A) does it: revenue depends on capacity and volume, not just rating; the top-rated places are tiny and thus low-revenue despite high ratings, while 4.0-star places are large and high-volume. This directly explains the revenue gap. (B) predicts the opposite of the observation. (C) and (D) concern how ratings are shown or earned, not revenue. (E) number of reviews does not determine revenue.

items.stem: During a drought, a county asked all households to cut water use. Afterward, the county reported that residential water CONSUMPTION dropped by 15 percent, yet the water utility's total residential REVENUE rose. Which of the following, if true, best explains how revenue could rise while consumption fell?
choices: A) Households reduced their water use mainly by watering lawns less often.
B) To offset the expected loss from conservation, the utility raised its per-gallon water rate by an amount more than enough to cover the drop in usage.
C) The drought lasted longer than the county had predicted.
D) Commercial water users were not asked to conserve.
E) The utility spent more on drought-awareness advertising during the period.
items.explanation: Revenue = price x quantity. Quantity fell 15 percent, so for revenue to rise, price must have risen by more than enough to offset the volume drop. (B) states exactly this, resolving the paradox. (A) explains how consumption fell but not how revenue rose. (C) drought duration does not by itself raise revenue. (D) concerns commercial users, but the figures are residential. (E) higher spending affects costs/profit, not revenue.

items.stem: A gardening magazine noted a surprising pattern: the tomato variety it rated the tastiest is grown by far fewer home gardeners than a variety it rated only average in taste. This is puzzling because home gardeners say taste is the most important factor in choosing what to grow. The pattern is best explained if which of the following completes the passage? The tastiest variety __________.
choices: A) was introduced to the market only very recently and is still little known.
B) produces fruit that most gardeners find delicious.
C) is more expensive to buy as seeds than the average-rated variety.
D) is highly susceptible to common diseases and very difficult to grow to maturity in home gardens.
E) has been rated highly by the magazine for several years.
items.explanation: To explain why the tastiest variety is grown less despite taste being the top priority, we need a strong offsetting drawback. (D) supplies it: if the tasty variety is very hard to grow successfully at home, gardeners avoid it despite its taste - resolving the paradox. (A) mere recency is weaker and does not fit 'far fewer' as decisively; the average variety could also be new. (B) restates the taste advantage, deepening the puzzle. (C) a modest seed-price difference is a weak deterrent compared with a cultivation failure. (E) longevity of the rating would, if anything, increase adoption.

items.stem: Economists predicted that when Grendon County raised its minimum wage, the number of teenagers holding jobs there would fall sharply. Instead, one year later teenage employment in Grendon County was slightly higher than before. Which of the following, if true, would most help to explain why the prediction failed?
choices: A) The minimum-wage increase was smaller than increases enacted in neighboring counties.
B) The economists had correctly predicted employment effects in other counties.
C) Some Grendon employers reduced the hours of their existing teenage workers.
D) The county's total population grew slightly over the year.
E) In the same year, a large new distribution center opened in Grendon County and hired hundreds of teenage workers to meet surging demand.
items.explanation: The prediction (higher wage -> fewer teen jobs) failed. An explanation must supply a force that increased teen employment enough to outweigh any wage effect. (B) does so directly: a large new employer created hundreds of teen jobs. (A) a smaller increase does not explain an actual rise, only a milder decline. (C) the economists' accuracy elsewhere does not explain Grendon's outcome. (D) fewer hours per worker does not raise the number of teens employed - if anything the reverse. (E) slight population growth is too weak and unspecific to teens or jobs.

items.stem: A hospital administrator was puzzled: Surgeon Reyes has a lower patient survival rate than Surgeon Okafor, yet the administrator insists Reyes may well be the more skilled surgeon. Which of the following, if true, best supports the administrator's position by explaining the survival-rate gap without attributing it to lesser skill?
choices: A) Because of Reyes's reputation, Reyes is regularly assigned the most complex and high-risk cases that other surgeons decline.
B) Reyes performs the same routine operations that Okafor performs.
C) Okafor has performed more operations in total than Reyes has.
D) Both surgeons trained at the same medical school.
E) Reyes's survival rate has been stable for several years.
items.explanation: The apparent contradiction is 'lower survival rate' versus 'possibly more skilled.' (B) resolves it via case mix (selection effect): Reyes handles the hardest, riskiest cases, which naturally have lower survival regardless of skill, so a lower raw rate is consistent with greater skill. (A) equal case difficulty would remove the explanation, undermining the position. (C) volume does not explain the rate gap. (D) shared training does not address why rates differ. (E) stability of the rate says nothing about difficulty of cases or skill.

items.stem:
City planner: Some residents claim that installing more streetlights on Maple Avenue would waste money. But **nighttime accidents on Maple Avenue are three times as frequent as on comparably busy, well-lit streets.** Therefore, the city should fund additional streetlights on Maple Avenue.

In the argument, the boldface portion plays which of the following roles?
choices: A) It is the main conclusion the planner is arguing for.
B) It is evidence offered in support of the planner's conclusion.
C) It is a claim the planner concedes but ultimately rejects.
D) It restates the objection that the planner opposes.
E) It is an assumption that the planner leaves unstated.
items.explanation: The final sentence ('Therefore, the city should fund additional streetlights') is the conclusion. The boldface accident statistic is the reason given for that conclusion, so it is supporting evidence. (A) names the wrong sentence as the conclusion. (C) is wrong because the planner uses, not rejects, the fact. (D) is wrong because the objection is the residents' 'waste money' claim, which is stated separately. (E) is wrong because the statistic is explicitly stated, not unstated.

items.stem:
Manufacturer's spokesperson: Environmental groups insist that our new packaging is no greener than the old kind. **It is true that the new packaging uses the same amount of plastic as the old.** However, the new packaging is fully recyclable, whereas the old packaging was not, so overall it is far better for the environment.

In the argument, the boldface portion plays which of the following roles?
choices: A) It is the conclusion the spokesperson is defending.
B) It is the position the spokesperson sets out to establish.
C) It is the primary evidence supporting the spokesperson's conclusion.
D) It is a point that the spokesperson concedes to the critics before arguing against their overall position.
E) It is an objection that the spokesperson dismisses as false.
items.explanation: The spokesperson grants ('It is true that...') a point favorable to the critics, then pivots with 'However' to argue the packaging is still greener overall. That granted point is a concession. (A) and (B) are wrong: the conclusion is that the new packaging is better for the environment. (D) is wrong because the recyclability point, not the plastic-amount point, is the supporting evidence. (E) is wrong because the spokesperson accepts the point as true rather than dismissing it.

items.stem:
Analyst: Executives at Vertrow assumed that lowering ticket prices would boost total revenue, since more people would attend concerts. **But when prices were cut last year, attendance rose only slightly while revenue fell sharply.** This shows that the executives' assumption was mistaken, and the company should abandon further price cuts.

In the argument, the two boldface portions play which of the following roles? (The first boldface is the sentence beginning 'But when prices...'; the second is 'the company should abandon further price cuts.')
choices: A) The first is a prediction the analyst endorses; the second is the evidence for that prediction.
B) The first is the main conclusion; the second is a subordinate conclusion that follows from it.
C) The first states the executives' assumption; the second is the analyst's objection to it.
D) The first and the second are both intermediate conclusions supporting an unstated main conclusion.
E) The first is evidence that undermines a position; the second is the main conclusion drawn partly from that evidence.
items.explanation: The first boldface reports last year's result, which undermines the executives' assumption. From that (plus the finding that the assumption was mistaken), the analyst draws the recommendation in the second boldface, which is the argument's main conclusion. So: evidence against a position, then main conclusion. (A) mislabels the first as a prediction. (C) reverses which part is the conclusion. (D) wrongly calls the first the executives' assumption; the assumption is stated in the first sentence, not the boldface. (E) is wrong because the second boldface is the main conclusion, not an intermediate one.

items.stem:
Columnist: A popular theory holds that reducing class sizes always improves student test scores. **In the Fairhaven district, class sizes were cut by a third, yet average test scores did not change.** The theory's defenders respond that Fairhaven simply hired too many inexperienced teachers during the transition. If that response is correct, then the flat scores in Fairhaven do not actually count against the theory.

In the argument, the boldface portion plays which of the following roles?
choices: A) It is a finding whose apparent force against a theory the argument goes on to question.
B) It is the columnist's main conclusion about the theory.
C) It is an assumption made by the theory's defenders.
D) It is evidence that the columnist uses to reject the theory outright.
E) It is a general principle from which the columnist reasons.
items.explanation: The boldface is the Fairhaven finding that seems to contradict the class-size theory, but the argument then presents the defenders' response and concludes that, if correct, the finding does not count against the theory. So the argument questions the finding's apparent force. (B) is wrong: the columnist draws no firm conclusion against the theory. (C) is wrong: it is a stated observation, not the defenders' assumption. (D) is wrong because the columnist does not reject the theory; the argument casts doubt on the finding. (E) is wrong because it is a specific datum, not a general principle.

items.stem:
Economist: Many argue that raising the minimum wage causes small businesses to lay off workers. **If that were generally true, we would expect regions that raised their minimum wage to show rising unemployment relative to regions that did not.** Yet across the past decade, no such difference in unemployment has appeared. Therefore, the claim that minimum-wage increases cause layoffs is not well supported.

In the argument, the boldface portion plays which of the following roles?
choices: A) It is the main conclusion of the economist's argument.
B) It states a prediction that would follow from a claim the economist argues against, and whose failure the economist uses to weaken that claim.
C) It is direct evidence establishing that minimum-wage increases do not cause layoffs.
D) It is an objection raised by opponents of raising the minimum wage.
E) It is a premise the economist concedes to be true and builds the conclusion upon.
items.explanation: The boldface spells out what we would observe IF the layoff claim were true (a conditional prediction). The next sentence reports that this prediction did not hold, and the economist uses that failure to weaken the layoff claim. (A) is wrong: the conclusion is the last sentence. (C) is wrong because the boldface is a hypothetical prediction, not observed evidence. (D) is wrong because it is the economist's own reasoning, not the opponents' objection. (E) is wrong because the boldface is not asserted as true; it is a conditional whose consequent is later shown to fail.

items.stem:
Two researchers debate a study:

Pro: The study found that people who take daily walks report better moods, so daily walking must improve mood.

Con: **The study only surveyed people who already walk daily; it never compared them with otherwise similar people who do not walk.** For all we know, cheerful people are simply more inclined to take walks in the first place.

In Con's response, the boldface portion plays which of the following roles?
choices: A) It is Con's main conclusion that walking does not improve mood.
B) It is a concession that Pro's conclusion is correct.
C) It identifies a limitation in the study's design that Con uses to challenge Pro's inference.
D) It is the evidence Pro cited in support of the original claim.
E) It is an alternative explanation Con proposes for the study's results.
items.explanation: Con points out that the study lacked a comparison group, a design limitation, and uses it to challenge Pro's causal inference. (A) overstates: Con does not conclude walking has no effect, only that Pro's inference is unsupported. (C) is the opposite of Con's move. (D) is wrong: Pro's cited evidence is the mood/walking correlation, not the design limitation. (E) is wrong: the alternative explanation ('cheerful people walk more') is the following sentence, not the boldface.

items.stem:
Restaurant owner: We should switch to the new reservation app. **The app automatically reminds customers the day before their booking.** Restaurants that use such reminders report far fewer no-shows, and no-shows are our biggest source of lost revenue.

In the argument, the boldface portion plays which of the following roles?
choices: A) It is the main conclusion of the argument.
B) It is an objection to switching to the new app.
C) It is a prediction about the restaurant's future revenue.
D) It is a fact about the app that the owner uses as a premise in support of the recommendation.
E) It is an assumption the owner makes without stating it.
items.explanation: The recommendation ('We should switch to the new app') is the conclusion. The boldface states a feature of the app (automatic reminders) that, combined with the reminder/no-show link, supports the recommendation, so it is a supporting premise. (A) names the wrong sentence. (C) is wrong because the fact favors switching. (D) is wrong because it states a current feature, not a prediction. (E) is wrong because the fact is explicitly stated.

items.stem:
Policy adviser: Opponents of the new subway line argue that it will not reduce road congestion. **They point out that when a similar line opened in Brill City, highway traffic there did not fall.** But Brill City added the line at the same time it eliminated highway tolls, which drew more drivers onto the roads. In our city, no such change is planned, so the subway line can be expected to ease congestion.

In the argument, the boldface portion plays which of the following roles?
choices: A) It is the adviser's main conclusion.
B) It is a premise the adviser uses directly to support the conclusion that the subway will ease congestion.
C) It is the adviser's concession that the subway line will not work.
D) It is an assumption shared by both the adviser and the opponents.
E) It is evidence offered by the opponents, which the adviser then argues is undermined by a special feature of the cited case.
items.explanation: The boldface is the opponents' supporting evidence (the Brill City example). The adviser then argues that Brill City was atypical because tolls were eliminated, undermining the opponents' use of that evidence. (A) is wrong: the conclusion is the last sentence. (C) is wrong because the adviser attacks this evidence rather than relying on it. (D) is wrong because the adviser does not concede the subway will fail. (E) is wrong because it is the opponents' point, which the adviser disputes as applied.

items.stem:
Biologist: The invasive beetle has been blamed for the recent decline of oak trees in the valley. **However, oak decline began two years before the beetle was first detected there.** A prolonged drought, which started three years ago, is the more likely cause. The valley's oaks should therefore be treated for drought stress rather than for beetle infestation.

In the argument, the boldface portion plays which of the following roles?
choices: A) It is evidence that casts doubt on one explanation and thereby supports the biologist's preferred explanation.
B) It is the main conclusion about how the oaks should be treated.
C) It is the explanation the biologist ultimately endorses.
D) It is a concession that the beetle is responsible for the decline.
E) It is a general principle about invasive species.
items.explanation: The timing fact (decline began before the beetle appeared) undercuts the beetle explanation and lends support to the drought explanation the biologist favors. (A) is wrong: the treatment recommendation is the conclusion. (C) is wrong because the endorsed explanation is drought, stated separately. (D) is the opposite of what the boldface does. (E) is wrong because it is a specific timing fact, not a general principle.

items.stem:
Museum director: To increase attendance, some board members want us to lower ticket prices. **Lower prices would indeed bring in more visitors on any given day.** But our galleries are already at capacity on weekends, so more visitors would only worsen crowding and drive away members. We should instead extend our hours, spreading visitors across more of the week.

In the argument, the two boldface portions play which of the following roles? (The first boldface is 'Lower prices would indeed bring in more visitors on any given day.'; the second is 'We should instead extend our hours, spreading visitors across more of the week.')
choices: A) The first is the director's main conclusion; the second is evidence for it.
B) The first is a claim the director accepts but argues is outweighed by other considerations; the second is the director's own recommendation.
C) The first is an objection the director rejects as false; the second is a concession to the board.
D) The first and second are both recommendations the director endorses.
E) The first is evidence for the board's proposal; the second is the reason the director gives against extending hours.
items.explanation: The director grants that lower prices would raise daily visitors (first boldface) but argues this is outweighed by capacity/crowding problems, then offers an alternative recommendation, extending hours (second boldface). So: accepted-but-outweighed point, then the director's own conclusion. (A) reverses roles. (C) is wrong: the director accepts the first as true and the second is not a concession. (D) is wrong: the first is the board's point, not the director's recommendation. (E) mislabels the second as a reason against extending hours, which is actually the director's recommendation.

items.stem:
Editor: A reporter claims our readership is falling because our articles are too long. **Our average article length has in fact grown by 20 percent over the past year.** Yet during that same year, our total readership rose steadily. So the reporter's explanation cannot be right.

In the argument, the boldface portion plays which of the following roles?
choices: A) It is the main conclusion that the reporter's explanation is wrong.
B) It is the reporter's explanation for the falling readership.
C) It is a factual premise that, combined with another fact, is used to reject the reporter's explanation.
D) It is an assumption the editor fails to support.
E) It is a prediction about future article length.
items.explanation: The editor establishes two facts, that article length grew (boldface) and that readership also rose, and uses the pair to reject the reporter's claim that longer articles drove readers away. The boldface is one of those factual premises. (A) is wrong: the conclusion is the last sentence. (C) is wrong: the reporter's explanation is that long articles cause falling readership, stated in the first sentence. (D) is wrong because it is a stated fact, not an unsupported assumption. (E) is wrong because it reports a past change, not a prediction.

items.stem:
Consultant: Management believes that offering remote work will cut office costs. **Remote work would let the firm give up half of its leased floor space, an immediate saving.** However, the firm's lease runs for six more years and cannot be reduced, so no space savings are possible until then. Meanwhile, supporting remote work would require costly new equipment now. In the near term, therefore, remote work would raise the firm's costs, not lower them.

In the argument, the boldface portion plays which of the following roles?
choices: A) It is the consultant's main conclusion.
B) It is a premise the consultant uses to support the conclusion that costs will fall.
C) It is an objection that the consultant raises against management and immediately accepts.
D) It expresses the reasoning behind a belief that the consultant goes on to argue does not hold in the near term.
E) It is evidence that directly proves remote work will raise costs.
items.explanation: The boldface lays out the rationale behind management's belief that remote work saves money (giving up floor space). The consultant then argues this cannot happen in the near term because the lease is fixed and new equipment is needed, concluding costs will actually rise. So the boldface is the reasoning behind a belief the consultant refutes for the near term. (A) is wrong: the conclusion is the last sentence. (C) is wrong: the consultant's conclusion is that costs rise, opposite to this. (D) is wrong because the consultant does not accept it; he rebuts it. (E) is wrong because the boldface supports the cost-cutting view, not the cost-raising conclusion.

items.stem:
Coach: Fans say we lose close games because our players lack stamina late in matches. **Our players' measured fitness scores are among the highest in the league.** So the problem is not stamina. What we truly lack is a reliable set play for the final minutes, and that is what we must develop.

In the argument, the two boldface portions play which of the following roles? (The first boldface is 'Our players' measured fitness scores are among the highest in the league.'; the second is 'that is what we must develop.')
choices: A) The first is the main conclusion; the second is a premise supporting it.
B) The first is the fans' explanation; the second is the coach's objection to it.
C) The first and second are both pieces of evidence for the same conclusion.
D) The first is a concession to the fans; the second is evidence that stamina is the real problem.
E) The first is evidence against a proposed explanation; the second is the course of action the coach concludes should be taken.
items.explanation: The high fitness scores (first boldface) are evidence against the fans' stamina explanation.
